ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuing increase in water pollution remains a serious threat to public health and environmental sustainability [1,2]. In 2021, a total of 10,683 rural and urban villages in Indonesia were affected by water contamination [2–4]. Furthermore, industrial wastewater contains harmful dyes like methylene orange (MO), which requires specialized treatment due to its high colorant concentration [2]. MO is an organic azo dye with a complex chemical structure, commonly used in the food, cosmetic, plastic, and textile industries [3,4]. However, MO dye consist of hazardous and toxic materials that can negatively affect living organisms and the environment, potentially causing carcinogenic and mutagenic effects on human health [5]. Several conventional methods including chemical, biological, and physical processing are utilized to treat wastewater before it is released into the environment [6]. Nevertheless, MO is among the most stable dyes, posing substantial challenges for degradation and often rendering conventional treatment methods ineffective and energy-intensive. Thus, enhanced treatment techniques including adsorption, biodegradation, and photocatalyst degradation are constantly being developed to effectively treat wastewater [7–10].

Photocatalytic technology is considered a highly promising solution due to its eco-friendliness, efficiency, and exceptional stability [11,12]. This approach integrates solar energy to reduce or oxidize inorganic pollutants, converting them into non-toxic substances [11–13]. Photocatalysts are widely utilized for the degradation of organic contaminants in water, the inactivation of pathogenic bacteria, and the enhancement of water purification processes [14]. Photocatalysts commonly use semiconductor materials such as CuO [15], ZnO [16], CdS [17], CdSe [18], and TiO2 [19]. Among these, titanium dioxide (TiO2) is particularly desirable as a catalyst material due to its high catalytic activity, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and antibacterial properties [20]. However, the photocatalytic performance of TiO₂ is often limited by the recombination of electron-hole pairs [21]. Furthermore, TiO₂ has a band gap of ∼3.20 eV, which confines its high reactivity to UV light and limits its effectiveness under visible sunlight [21]. To overcome this limitation, researchers have developed various strategies to enhance the photocatalytic performance of TiO2. These strategies include modifying TiO₂ via incorporating additional semiconductor materials, doping with metal or non-metal ions, and compositing with carbon materials [22,23]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have garnered considerable attention for their applications in nanomedicine [24], photocatalysis [25], and sensing technologies [26]. CQDs offer numerous advantages, including ease of modification, excellent biocompatibility, superior optical properties, low toxicity, and environmental sustainability [27,28]. Importantly, CQDs enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 due to their exceptional electron transfer and light-harvesting capabilities [29]. The integration of TiO2 with CQDs improves photocatalytic performance under visible light and reduces electron-hole recombination in TiO2 [30,31]. Previous studies emphasize the substantial potential of TiO2 and CQDs as advanced photocatalytic materials [28–32].

Earlier, Tong et al. [33] used the sol-gel hydrolysis method to synthesize CQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites (NCs), achieving a degradation efficiency of 85.47 % for rhodamine B (Rh(B)) in aqueous solutions. In addition, Jin et al. [34] developed a method to composite TiO2 with CQDs obtained from coffee powder using a hydrothermal calcination process. This combination exhibited a degradation rate for methylene blue (MB) that was 2.25 times more than that of pure TiO2, achieving an outstanding efficiency of 95.8 % [34]. Mozdbar et al. [35] demonstrated that the degradation performance of CQDs/TiO2 was 2.4 times greater than pure TiO2 NPs. Yet, the precise composition of CQDs in TiO2 NCs matrix for effective MO degradation remains underexplored. MO was chosen due to its distinctive orange hue and its prevalence in textile industry waste as an azo dye. The azo bond in the MO molecule serves as a crucial target for degradation in photocatalytic reactions, which resembles the chemical structure of other toxic organic compounds prevalent in the environment [36]. MO exhibits strong light absorption at nearly 465 nm, facilitating its detection and quantification with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. This characteristic simplifies the monitoring concentration changes during the degradation process. This study aims to optimize CQD-TiO2 NCs to achieve superior optical properties, morphology, NC size, and pore dimensions for enhanced MO degradation. We develop a simple and cost-effective hydrothermal synthesis method to integrate CQDs into TiO2 NCs and examine their impact on photocatalytic performance. The synthesized CQD-TiO2 NCs were characterized using various analytical tools including TEM, PL, UV–Vis, BET, BJH, and pHzpc, measurements. The photocatalytic degradation of MO was evaluated and determined the degradation efficiency under visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and preparation of CQD-TiO2 NCs
ce:section.ce:para: CQD-TiO2 NCs were synthesized using citric acid (C₆H₈O₇, 99.9 %), urea (CH₄N₂O, 99.9 %), deionized water (DIW), titanium dioxide (TiO2, 99.9 %), and ethanol (C₂H₅OH, 99.5 %). All liquid media were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Citric acid was chosen due to its high carbon-rich composition and optimal carbon structure, which are required for the effective synthesis of CQDs. Meanwhile, urea was used as a nitrogen source to enhance the photoluminescent properties of the CQDs [10]. High-purity DIW is crucial in chemical reactions due to its ability to prevent ion interference. Methylene orange (MO, 99.5 % purity) was diluted with DIW to achieve a concentration of 10 ppm. CQDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method [35,37], 2 mmol of citric acid and 6 mmol of urea were dissolved in 25 mL of DIW and stirred at 450 rpm for 10 min to achieve a homogeneous solution. The solution was placed in an autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. Upon cooling to room temperature, it was removed from the autoclave, yielding CQDs in the form of a light green solution. For the synthesis of CQD-TiO2 NCs, 0.2 g of TiO2 were dissolved in 40 mL of DIW and 20 mL of ethanol. The mixture was then stirred for approximately 10 min to obtained a homogeneous solution. Next, 1.5 mL of the prepared CQD solution was added to the mixture and stirred it for an additional 5 h at room temperature. The solution was then transferred to an autoclave and heated at 100 °C for 8 h. The resulting sample was washed three times with DIW at room temperature. The precipitate was dried at 100 °C for approximately 2 h to remove residual water. This process was repeated with varying volumes of CQDs (1.5, 2, 2.5, and 3 mL). The preparation of the CQD-TiO2 NCs is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the prepared CQD-TiO2 NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphology of the CQD-TiO2 NCs was analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a JEOL JEM-2100 F. The obtained TEM images were processed using ImageJ software to determine the size of the CQD-TiO2 NCs. This analysis confirmed the decoration of the TiO2 surface with CQDs. The optical properties of the CQD-TiO2 NCs were observed through photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy using an Aurora 4000 system with an excitation wavelength of 420 nm. The PL spectra provided insights into the role of CQDs in the photocatalytic process. The specific surface area of the CQD-TiO2 NCs was measured using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis with a Micromeritics ASAP 2010. Photoelectrochemical responses were recorded with a CHI 660 B electrochemical workstation in a conventional three-electrode setup under visible light. The measured specific surface area was reported in m2/g, and the adsorption and desorption isotherms were analyzed to determine pore volume and pore size distribution. A hot filtration test was performed to filter the CQDs-TiO2 NCs after heating at 250 °C, ensuring the effective separation of solid and heterogeneous components. The NCs were then heated at 100 °C for 4 h to eliminate any residual moisture. The mass of the dried NCs was measured before and after the filtration process to quantify any changes. Furthermore, the pH of zero point charge (pHzpc) was assessed to determine the surface characteristics of the CQDs-TiO2 NCs and their interactions at various pH levels. The NC materials were dissolved in a solution containing 0.1 M NaCl and 0.1 M HCl with varying pH values at 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10. A total charge of zero indicates that the particle surface exhibits no net charge.

The photocatalytic performance of TiO2 and CQD-TiO2 NCs was evaluated by assessing the degradation of MO solution under visible light using a solar simulator. For this assessment, 0.1 g of the photocatalyst material was mixed with 50 mL of MO solution with a concentration of 10 ppm and exposed to the light for 150 min. The sample was positioned within 25 cm from the solar simulator. The experiment was conducted at ambient temperature to simulate environmental conditions and measure the photocatalyst effectiveness for practical applications. At 25-min intervals, the MO solution was taken and centrifuged to separate the catalyst powder from the liquid phase. The supernatant was then analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrometer to determine the residual MO concentration. A decrease in absorbance spectra indicates a reduction in MO levels. After the photodegradation process, the catalyst powder was isolated from the decolorized MO solution. The absorbance of the degraded MO solution was measured again using the UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The efficiency of each photocatalyst in degrading MO was determined using the following equation [33]:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystallinity of the prepared CQD-TiO2 NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The phase structure of TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2 NCs was examined using XRD analysis. It was revealed that TiO2 and CQDs-TiO2 NCs exhibit a tetragonal crystal structure with an anatase phase (space group I 41/AMD) [33]. Fig. 2 clarified that the diffraction peaks of CQDs-TiO2 NCs were observed at 2θ of 25.40°, 37.80°, 48.13°, 53.98°, 55.15°, and 62.75° (AMCSD No. 0019093) [38]. These peaks corresponded to the hkl indices (101), (004), (200), (105), (211), and (204), respectively. The presence of CQDs has no impact on the phase purity of TiO₂. CQDs are efficiently deposited on the TiO₂ surface and integrated into the TiO₂ crystal lattice. The crystal size of TiO2 was determined to be 45.51 nm; however, it decreased to 42.45 nm upon the addition of CQDs. The additional diffraction peaks observed are attributed to unobservable precursor materials [12,39]. The diffraction signals from CQDs are not detectable owing to their low concentration and limited crystallinity [40–43]. Consequently, TEM measurements were used to accurately identify and confirm the presence of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology of prepared CQD-TiO2 NCs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the CQD-TiO2 NCs was analyzed using TEM measurement. Fig. 3 (a–d) exemplified the morphological characteristics of the CQD-TiO2 NCs with varying concentrations of CQDs. The TEM images revealed that ultra-fine CQD particles measure between 5 and 10 nm with graphite phases attached to the surface of octahedron TiO2 NCs [27]. The larger particles correspond to TiO₂, whereas the smaller particles indicated by yellow circles are the CQDs adhering to the TiO₂ surfaces (Fig. 3, insets (a-d)). This observation confirmed the effective functionalization of TiO₂ particles with CQDs. Furthermore, the particle size of TiO2 was measured to be ∼40 nm. While the addition of CQDs led to an increase in the average particle size to around 100 nm. This enlargement is attributed to high temperature and pressure during synthesis and the formation of the CQD-TiO₂ NCs.

Furthermore, the charge trapping behavior was examined using PL analysis. It revealed that higher emission intensities indicate a greater recombination rate of photo-generated charges, while lower intensities show reduced recombination [44,45]. A stronger PL signal correlates with an increased amount of emitted light during the recombination process, signifying that more excited electrons are recombining with holes and releasing energy in the form of light. Fig. 5(a–e) displayed that the NBE emission intensity increases with the addition of CQDs, while the Tii intensity decreases. The NBE peak exhibits a slight shift towards the visible light region, implying a reduction in the band gap energy. The low emission intensity observed in the CQD-TiO₂ NC samples indicates a decreased recombination of photogenerated carriers due to an increase in oxygen vacancies, which serve as electron traps [31,32]. In addition, CQDs deposited on the TiO2 surface efficiently inhibit photo-induced electron recombination, acting as charge separators and increasing the electron-hole lifetime compared to pure TiO₂ [30]. TiO₂ itself cannot be excited to produce electrons under visible light irradiation, whereas CQDs can be excited and act as electron donors with TiO₂ serving as the electron acceptor [46]. Table 1 summarizes the NBE, Tii, and V0 that reveal relatively low electron-hole recombination which can enhance photocatalytic activity. PL induces electron excitation from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB) [47]. The CB and VB values can be determined using Equations (2) and (3) [48], where Ee represents the electron-free energy on the hydrogen scale and Eg denotes the band gap energy. In Equation (4), X signifies the electronegativity of atoms A and B, while a and b are their atomic numbers. According to these calculations, TiO₂ has CB and VB values of −3.48 eV and −0.28 eV, respectively, while CQD has CB and VB values of −3.2 eV and −0.7 eV. These findings indicate that the band gaps of the materials are responsive to visible light [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface area and porosity of CQD-TiO2 NCs

ce:section.ce:para:
The photocatalytic performance of TiO2 and CQD-TiO2 NCs was assessed under visible light using a solar simulator with irradiation times of 0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 125, and 150 min. Fig. 7 (a) exposed the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 and CQD-TiO2 NCs with varying concentrations of CQDs. The CQD-TiO2 NCs demonstrated superior photocatalytic performance compared to pure TiO2. After 150 min of visible light exposure, TiO2 achieved an 80.64 % degradation of MO. Pure TiO2 exhibited lower photocatalytic activity under visible light, revealing that it is more effective under UV light, where it can degrade methylene blue (MB) dye up to 96 % [46]. In contrast, the samples (V1–V3) of CQD-TiO2 NCs demonstrated enhanced degradation efficiencies for MO with variations in CQD concentrations, achieving degradation rates of 94.32 %, 94.69 %, and 96.01 %, respectively. Among these, the sample (V4) exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity with 99.00 % degradation, attributable to its superior pore volume (Fig. 7(a) and Table 3 ). Fig. 7 (a, inset) illustrates the decoloartion of MO using CQD-TiO2 NCs. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of CQD-TiO2 NCs is due to the interaction between CQDs and TiO2. Furthermore, the improvement in photocatalytic performance could be attributed to the efficient transfer of electrons and holes. Hence, CQDs enhance charge transfer within the photocatalyst, allowing it to achieve optimal absorption of visible light [56]. In a photocatalytic reaction, electrons and holes particularly contribute to chemical processes rather than recombining. A reduced recombination rate enhances their interactions with surrounding molecules, promoting the generation of ROS and subsequently enabling the degradation of pollutants or organic substances [57,58]. Fig. 7(b) elucidated the photocatalytic mechanism, demonstrating how ROS are generated and utilized to decompose organic pollutants. Titanium cations (Ti4+) act as catalysts during the photodegradation process, producing hydroxyl radicals (·OH), which effectively break down organic compounds. In addition, anions play a role in determining the efficiency of dye degradation [59–62].

The Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) kinetic model was used to measure the photodegradation reaction on the surface of TiO2 and CQD-TiO2 NCs under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic degradation reactions of TiO2 and CQD-TiO2 NCs variations were analyzed via pseudo-first-order kinetics as shown in Fig. 8 . The representation of ln(C

The reaction constants for TiO₂ and various CQD-TiO₂ NCs initially increased with the addition of CQDs but then decreased for the V4 sample. These observations revealed that the experimental data do not align with a first-order kinetic model. Consequently, a second-order kinetic model is more accurately characterizing the reaction and mechanism. Fig. 9 presents the analysis of the nonlinear plot of t/qt versus irradiation time, illustrating both the reaction constant and the second-order regression coefficient. This analysis is based on a pseudo-second-order kinetic model. The pseudo-second-order kinetics are described by Equation (7) (see Fig. 9).

Table 3 shows that the regression coefficients for all samples are close to one, indicating that the kinetic data better fits the nonlinear second-order kinetic equation rather than the pseudo first-order model. Catalyst dosage and CQD concentration play important roles in enhancing photodegradation performance [63,64]. The observed increase in MO degradation efficiency can be attributed to the greater obtainability of active material with higher CQD concentrations. Furthermore, higher catalyst dosage generally improves photodegradation outcomes. Thus, the concentration of CQDs was meticulously optimized in conjunction with the TiO₂-CQD NCs to achieve the most effective photocatalytic degradation. The pH level plays a crucial role in determining the degradation efficiency of MO. Numerous studies demonstrated that adjusting the pH from acidic to alkaline conditions can significantly increase photocatalytic activity for dye decolorization [65–67]. The pHzpc value is crucial when the surface charge of the NCs becomes neutral. When the pH exceeds the pHzpc, the surface of CQD-TiO₂ NCs acquires a net negative charge. This can enhance the adsorption of hydroxyl ions (OH⁻), which are precursors to highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH). These radicals play a significant role in the photocatalytic degradation of MO. Thus, the pHzpc value is crucial for optimizing the photocatalytic performance of the CQD-TiO₂ NCs under varying pH conditions. Besides, the integration of CQDs enhanced the absorption of visible light, further improving the photocatalytic performance. The addition of CQDs into TiO₂ extends the light absorption spectrum, enabling the utilization of a broader range of light energy to activate the photocatalytic process due to the generation of greater electron-hole pairs. Moreover, particle photo-stability pertains to the retention of the material structure and composition throughout and after the photocatalytic process [68,69]. A stable photocatalyst maintains its integrity without releasing ions into the environment upon light excitation, while unstable materials may discharge ions during the process. Hoffmann et al. demonstrated that TiO₂ exhibits excellent thermal stability, making it an ideal candidate for the environmental degradation of dye pollutants [70]. Similarly, the stability of CQD-TiO₂ NCs is vital for their photocatalytic efficiency. Shafique et al. found that, even after multiple reuse cycles, the degradation efficiency of CQD-TiO₂ NCs decreased by only 8.5 % [71]. This slight decrease indicates that the availability of photocatalytic performance remains largely unaffected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hot filtration test and pHzpc evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 (a) exemplifies the pHfinal as a function of pHinitial for the point of zero charge (pHzpc) of TiO2 and CQD-TiO2 NCs prepared at various concentrations. It reveals that the pHzpc value for TiO2 is 6.2, while the pHzpc of CQD-TiO₂ NCs falls within the range of 5.6–6.0. This variation confirms the differences in surface charge characteristics between the two materials, which greatly influence their interactions under different pH levels. TiO₂ demonstrates a positive charge at pH < 6.2 and a negative charge at pH > 6.2. Conversely, the CQD-TiO₂ NCs are positively charged at pH < 5.6 and negatively charged at pH > 6, indicating an increase in the weak acidic nature of these NCs. Furthermore, the presence of negatively charged functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH) can further affect the surface properties of CQD-TiO₂ NCs. The pHzpc values of these CQD-TiO₂ NCs are crucial for determining electron transfer efficiency during photocatalytic reactions. On top, sample V4 exhibits the most efficient photocatalytic activity, confirming that the environmental pH associated with its pHzpc value effectively enhanced the degradation of MO. This proves that optimizing pH is an important role for improving the photocatalytic efficiency of CQD-TiO₂ NCs. Besides, Fig. 10 (b) displays the mass variations of TiO2 and CQD-TiO2 NCs at different concentrations measured through a hot filtration analysis. Upon hot filtration, the mass of these materials revealed a reduction of 5–15 %, attributed to the dissolution of the catalyst into the liquid phase [72]. This observation indicates that the photocatalytic reaction encompasses both heterogeneous and homogeneous mechanisms, clarifying the complex interplay between the catalyst and the reaction medium.

The CQD-TiO₂ NCs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method at varying concentrations. Various analytical tools including XRD, TEM, PL, BET, BJH, pHzpc, hot filtration and photoelectrochemical measurements were used to characterize the TiO₂ and CQD-TiO₂ NCs. TEM analysis confirmed uniform deposition of CQDs on the TiO₂ surface, yielding CQD-TiO₂ NCs with an average particle size of ∼100 nm. The surface area characteristics disclosed that the V3 sample with 2.5 mL addition of CQDs exhibited the highest specific surface area. Conversely, V4 sample with 3 mL addition of CQDs unveiled the largest pore diameter. These photocatalyst of NCs revealed exceptional performance up to 99.00 % degradation of MO under visible light due to their superior pore volume correlates with increased photocatalytic efficiency. The advancement of TiO₂-CQDs NCs photocatalysts obtains substantial promise for revolutionizing sustainable technologies. Furthermore, this study demonstrated that the utilization of visible light-assisted photocatalysts using CQD-TiO₂ NCs could be an effective solution for treating wastewater from the textile industry. However, achieving cost-effective production and optimizing the photocatalytic mechanism of CQDs-TiO₂ NCs are crucial for realizing their high potential in photocatalytic applications. In addition, the long-term stability and reusability of TiO₂-CQDs NCs require further analysis to ensure their practical viability.

Asrianti BT Sunardi: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Fadhillah Choirunnisa: Data Curation, Investigation. Atika S.P. Dewi: Data analysis and interpretation. Hendri Widiyandari: Conceptualization, Methodology. Yayuk Astuti: Writing and Review manuscript. Osi Arutanti: Validation and analysis data. Ali A Salim: Writing, Supervision, and Methodology. Nandang Mufti: Conceptualization, Writing and Supervision.

Indeed, it is not currently submitted for review to any other journal and will not be submitted elsewhere before a decision is made by your journal. We further, state that the submission of the manuscript has been approved by all co-authors.
